request.question: What year was the man whose car failed to arrive to the 1950 Indianapolis 500 first the Italian champion?

title: Giuseppe Farina

sentences: Dottore Emilio Giuseppe "Nino" Farina (30 October 1906 – 30 June 1966), was an Italian racing driver and was the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.
He was also the Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.
During his thirty-year racing career he suffered a series of accidents.
Jeff Wood (born January 20, 1957) is an American former race car driver born in Wichita, Kansas.
He was Formula Atlantic Rookie of the Year in 1977.
He drove in the CanAm series for Carl Haas in 1981 and finished 5th in the series behind Danny Sullivan.
In 1982, driving for Bob Garnetson Racing, finished 3rd place in the 24 Hours of Daytona.
He then made his CART debut in 1983 at the Caesars Palace Grand Prix driving for Dan Gurney.
He then drove in the Formula Atlantic West Series where he won the 1985 championship.
He returned to CART in 1987 and made 4 starts for Dick Simon with his best finish being 10th.
He was away from the series in 1988, then returned in 1989 to make 5 starts for Gohr Racing, where he finished 12th at the Michigan 500.
In 1990 he drove for a new team, Todd Walther Racing, where he made his first attempt to qualify for the Indianapolis 500, but crashed in practice.
He made 10 other starts, with a 10th-place finish at Phoenix, finishing 22nd in points.
In 1991 he made 8 starts for Dale Coyne Racing, Arciero Racing, and Euromotorsports with the best finish being 13th.
7 starts in '92 for Arciero netted Wood a 12th-place finish and a single point.
In 1993 Wood attempted a nearly full season for Andrea Moda Formula/Euromotorsports but failed to qualify for 6 races and only made 8 starts and again failed to score points.
He made four more unsuccessful starts in 1994 for Euromotorsports in what would be his final races in the series.
He was named to an entry for the 1996 Indianapolis 500, but the car failed to appear.
His best finish in his 49 CART races was an 8th place that came in his second series start back in 1983 at Laguna Seca Raceway.
Olson was a racing car constructor.
Olson cars competed in one round of the FIA World Championship - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Snowberger was a racing car constructor.
Snowberger cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Wetteroth was a racing car constructor.
Wetteroth cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Charles Van Acker (14 March 1912 Brussels – 31 May 1998 South Bend, Indiana) was a Belgian-American racecar driver.
He first attempted to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 in 1946 but was too slow.
In 1947 he made the race and finished in 29th after a crash on lap 24.
He also competed in seven more races of the national trail that season and finished 4th in points.
In 1948 he 11th in the Indy 500 and 10th in the National Championship.
1949 saw him crash 10 laps into the Indy 500 and struggle to qualify much of the rest of the season.
He attempted the 1950 Indianapolis 500 but failed to qualify in what would be his last Championship Car appearance.
Langley was an American racing car constructor.
Langley cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Andy Michner (born October 27, 1968, Ann Arbor, Michigan), is a former driver in the Indy Racing League IndyCar Series, NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series and NASCAR Busch Series.
He is the current record holder of the world's fastest Sprint Car race at a United States Auto Club event in Phoenix, Arizona at 136.034 mph 1996.
Michner finished twice a runner-up to NASCAR'S Tony Stewart in United States Auto Club competition and has 19 USAC Wins.
He passed his Indy 500 Rookie Orientation Program but elected to not qualify for the race 1996 Indianapolis 500.
In 1996 & 1997, Michner ran a partial season in the NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series as Chevrolet's Development Driver.
He then returned to the Indy Car series in 1998 with Konica/Syan Racing and captured his career best finish of eighth place in his first race, the 1998 Indianapolis 500.
Michner then signed with Factory Riley&Scott Reebok Indycar Team where Michner led in the closing laps of the 1998 Texas Longhorn 500 but failed to finish due to an engine failure.
At Michigan International Speedway in August 1998, it was announced, Michner signed a 3-year contract to drive the Bayer Aleve, Coca-Cola Chevrolet in NASCAR's Busch Series for BACE Motorsports.
Michner suffered ultimately career ending injuries in October 1998 while testing at Homestead-Miami Speedway in a NASCAR Busch Series test.
He attempted to qualify for the 1999 Indianapolis 500 for Byrd Racing but failed to make the field due to rain.
He was named to a Logan Racing entry to two races in 2000 but the car did not appear at either race.
Josele Garza (born March 15, 1962 in Mexico City, Mexico) is a Mexican professional race car driver.
He started seven Indianapolis 500 races.
Garza was the 1981 Indianapolis 500 Rookie of the Year after rolling off sixth and finishing 23rd at the age of 19.
At 19 years, two months, and nine days, Garza is the second youngest driver to compete in the Indianapolis 500 and was not surpassed until 2003 by A.J. Foyt IV.
USAC rules at the time required drivers to be 21 years of age, but Garza's racing license listed him as being 22 years old.
Garza claims he does not know how the error occurred but he did not notify officials of the error.
He finished 10th in the 1984 Indianapolis 500.
He led 13 Indy 500 laps.
The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.
The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.
It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.
The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.
Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.
The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

Prefer section-level targets and tight searches over broad suggestions.

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` compact: one-line header plus a total of 1–4 brief lines (sentences or bullet lines) covering the factual spans, the missing bridge fact, and the prioritized retrieval directives.
- Preserve exact factual spans from the passages; when quoting, keep quotes very short (<= ~15 words) and only if verbatim in the passages.
- Do not assert a final answer or resolve facts here. If the passages do fully and unambiguously contain the required relation, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but still do not produce a final answer.
- If multiple candidate entities or variant names appear, enumerate the top 1–2 candidates and mark the name as ambiguous, stating what is unverified for each.
- Do not invent facts, hypothesize, or add extraneous context not grounded in the provided passages.

- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:

- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.

- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.

  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. - When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Follow these additional concise operational rules when integrating evidence:
- Prefer the context-preferred name form for the preserved first (answer) sentence; when choosing a canonical name for supporting sentences, prefer the context form if there is a name-variant conflict across inputs.
- If a passage explicitly contradicts a preserved context sentence, replace the preserved sentence with a passage-supported first sentence or note the contradiction concisely in that first sentence.
- For each supporting sentence, start with the chosen canonical name and include only attributes explicitly present in the provided passages; if the passages do not assert a requested status or attribute, state that absence briefly rather than inferring the attribute.
- Do not add fields, requests for more retrieval, or change the input/output interface. Keep the `summary` to 1–3 sentences exactly and avoid extra commentary or formatting.

candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add any new facts or external information.
- First run a mandatory binary detection: determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to extraction.

Conservative extractive tie-breaking (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- When a contiguous longer verbatim phrase in either summary strictly contains a shorter candidate (adds adjacent words that are part of the name or label), emit the longest contiguous verbatim phrase exactly as it appears in the summaries.
- For personal names where one verbatim form is a substring of another, prefer the longest verbatim personal-name span found in either summary; if multiple distinct spans tie for length, prefer the form from `summary_2`.
- If either summary presents an alternate token parenthetically immediately adjacent to a name/place (e.g., canonical short form in parentheses), emit that parenthetical token exactly as shown. If both summaries present parenthetical tokens, prefer the one in `summary_2`.
- If the summaries do not explicitly state the requested relation but exactly one candidate entity of the relevant role appears verbatim across the summaries and no summary contradicts the attribution, emit that single verbatim entity. If multiple distinct candidates appear or there is any contradiction, do not apply this rule and return the unresolved outcome.
- Prefer wording from `summary_2` only as a tie-breaker, except when the longest-contiguous-span or parenthetical-token rules require choosing a longer or parenthetical form present in `summary_1`.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest contiguous verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind"). If multiple adjacent phrases appear, prefer the longest contiguous phrase unless that creates contradiction.
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

Use only the provided summaries as evidence and follow the conservative tie-breaking rules above when extracting the final minimal verbatim span.

response.hop1_query: What year was the man whose car failed to arrive to the 1950 Indianapolis 500 first the Italian champion?
hop1_docs: 1950 Indianapolis 500 | The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.  The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.  It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.  The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.  Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.  The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

2001 Indianapolis 500 | The 85th Indianapolis 500 was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana on Sunday, May 27, 2001.  Race rookie Hélio Castroneves, a three-year veteran of the CART series, led the final 52 laps and won his first Indy 500.  Penske Racing swept 1st-2nd with Gil de Ferran the runner-up.  Winning car owner Roger Penske scored his 11th victory at the Indianapolis 500, and his first-ever 1-2 finish in the race.  It was a redemption from the team's previous attempt at Indy (1995) in which both of his cars failed to qualify; subsequently followed by a five-year absence (1996-2000) due to the open wheel "split."

Chuck Ciprich | Charles J. Ciprich (Born November 30, 1941) is an American former racing driver from Sayre, Pennsylvania.  A successful modified stock car racer who won numerous championships throughout the northeast, Ciprich tried his hand at Championship Car racing in 1983 and 1984.  He first attempted to qualify for the 1983 Indianapolis 500 in an unsophisticated Chevrolet powered Finley chassis fielded by Pace Racing.  He made his CART Champ Car debut later that year at Pocono Raceway but qualified near the back of the field and was knocked out after 38 laps by a transmission failure.  Ciprich and the Pace team arrived for the first oval race of the 1984 season at Phoenix International Raceway with a more advanced, yet year-old March-Cosworth combination yet failed to make the field.  Their attempt to qualify the car for the 1984 Indianapolis 500 was equally unsuccessful and Ciprich returned to Modifieds.  Ciprich retired some years later and now resides in Florida.

Charles Van Acker | Charles Van Acker (14 March 1912 Brussels – 31 May 1998 South Bend, Indiana) was a Belgian-American racecar driver.  He first attempted to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 in 1946 but was too slow.  In 1947 he made the race and finished in 29th after a crash on lap 24.  He also competed in seven more races of the national trail that season and finished 4th in points.  In 1948 he 11th in the Indy 500 and 10th in the National Championship.  1949 saw him crash 10 laps into the Indy 500 and struggle to qualify much of the rest of the season.  He attempted the 1950 Indianapolis 500 but failed to qualify in what would be his last Championship Car appearance.

Bob Frey | Robert C. Frey (born October 25, 1950) is a former American racing driver from Elyria, Ohio.  He was a notable sprint car driver who won some of the country's most prestigious races when he attempted to try his hand at CART Championship Car racing in 1980.  His first attempt to qualify for a race at the Pocono Raceway ended in a practice crash.  His first race came in September of that year at the Michigan International Speedway and he finished 17th.  The following year Frey notched his best CART finish of 13th at Phoenix International Raceway but then failed to qualify his eight-year-old Eagle-Offy for the 1981 Indianapolis 500.  He returned the week after the Indy 500 and drove in the Milwaukee Mile race, finishing 17th.  Frey returned in 1982 with a much newer 1981 Eagle chassis but failed to qualify for both the Indy 500 and the Milwaukee race, after which he returned to sprint cars.

1978 USAC Championship Car season | The 1978 USAC Championship Car season consisted of 18 races, beginning at Phoenix on March 18 and concluding at the same location on October 28.  The USAC National Champion was Tom Sneva and the Indianapolis 500 winner was Al Unser.  This was the last year before the first USAC/CART "Split".  By winning the Indianapolis 500, Pocono 500, and California 500, Al Unser swept the Indy car "Triple Crown", the only driver in history to do so.

Phil Caliva | Phil Caliva (born January 1, 1945 in Palermo, Sicily) is an Italian-born American racing driver who competed in the CART Championship Car series from 1979 to 1984.  After racing in the North American Formula Super Vee series in 1977 and 1978, he made his CART debut in 1979 driving for Spike Gehlhausen at Ontario Motor Speedway.  Caliva returned the next year and made five starts for Bill Alsup and tried but failed to qualify for the Indianapolis 500.  Caliva attempted a second time to qualify for the "500" for Alsup in 1981 but again failed to make the field.  He made 3 more starts later that year, including his best series finish of 8th place at the Michigan International Speedway in July.  He tried 3 races including the Indy 500 in 1982 but failed to qualify for all of them.  He tried the Indy 500 again in 1983 and missed the field again, but made the race at Riverside International Raceway in what would be his last Champ Car start.  He tried the Long Beach race and the Indy 500 again in 1984 but again missed both races.
response.summary_1:
Giuseppe Farina / 1950 Indianapolis 500
- Fact: passage states "Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived." (1950 Indianapolis 500, May 30, 1950).
- Possible bridge entity: Giuseppe Farina — passages do NOT state the year he became the first Italian champion (i.e., the year he became the first Italian World Drivers' Champion).
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order): 1) "Giuseppe Farina" Wikipedia page — lede/infobox and "Career"/"Championships" sections; 2) "1950 World Championship of Drivers" (or "1950 World Drivers' Championship") page — lede and "Final standings"/"Drivers' standings" section to confirm champion year.
hop2_docs: Giuseppe Farina | Dottore Emilio Giuseppe "Nino" Farina (30 October 1906 – 30 June 1966), was an Italian racing driver and was the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.  He was also the Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.  During his thirty-year racing career he suffered a series of accidents.
Giuseppe La Farina | Giuseppe La Farina (20 July 1815 in Messina – 5 September 1863 in Torino) was an influential leader of the Italian Risorgimento.

2000 Heineken Trophy – Women's Doubles | Silvia Farina Elia and Rita Grande were the defending champions, but Farina Elia did not compete this year.  Grande teamed up with Sabine Appelmans and lost in semifinals to Catherine Barclay and Karina Habšudová.

Formula One drivers from Italy | There have been 98 Formula One drivers from Italy including two World Drivers' Champions.  Giuseppe "Nino" Farina won the first ever World Drivers' Championship and Alberto Ascari was the first double world champion.  All three championships came in the early 1950s and very few Italian drivers have come close since Ascari's 1953 victory.  Four of the most experienced Grand Prix drivers in history come from Italy.
1996 Meta Styrian Open – Doubles | Silvia Farina and Andrea Temesvári were the defending champions but did not compete that year.
2005 J&S Cup – Doubles | Silvia Farina Elia and Francesca Schiavone were the defending champions, but they chose not to compete that year together.
Gianluca Farina | Gianluca Farina (born 15 December 1962) is an Italian competition rower and Olympic champion.
response.summary_2: Giuseppe Farina was first the Italian Champion in 1937. Giuseppe Farina was also the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.
